evidence: goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go combines dependency type declarations, default wiring, cross-package runtime construction, and fallback error policy in one file, while goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_core.go layers another large merge/option system on top of the same composite runtime.

goseg/system/wifi_runtime_methods.go combines nmcli parsing, command execution, wifi client orchestration, interface toggling, and captive sysctl mutation in a single runtime surface; goseg/system/wifi_test.go and goseg/system/wifi_c2c_flow_test.go require broad seam scaffolding to isolate behaviors.

goseg/broadcast/state.go mixes mutable state ownership, event bus behavior, collector delegation, and manual deep-copy logic for nested broadcast payloads; goseg/broadcast/state_test.go has large mutation-heavy tests focused on clone coverage, signaling high coupling to internal representation.

goseg/config/config_update_schema.go merges patch schema declaration, parsing, apply logic, merge behavior, and init-time global registry validation; goseg/config/config_test.go correspondingly carries very large scenario-heavy tests to exercise intertwined concerns.

Publishing APIs use different failure contracts for similar queue/bus operations: `EventBroker.Publish*` returns explicit backpressure/cancel errors, while `LogstreamRuntime.PublishSystemLog` is fire-and-forget with no return channel for failure (`goseg/docker/events/events.go`, `goseg/session/logstream_session_store.go`).

Runtime APIs in adjacent subsystems expose inconsistent nil/missing-dependency behavior: some methods return typed dependency errors, others silently succeed/no-op (`goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_core.go`, `goseg/broadcast/state.go`).

WiFi query APIs return usable results plus non-nil warning errors in some paths, but this mixed contract is not uniformly normalized by callers (`goseg/system/wifi_runtime_methods.go`, `goseg/system/wifi_test.go`).

`StartupRuntime.Initialize()` returns `nil` when callback is unset, while sibling startup methods (`LoadWireguard`, `LoadMC`, `LoadUrbits`, etc.) return explicit missing-dependency errors, so the same runtime contract class has divergent semantics (`goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_core.go`).

`broadcastStateRuntime` methods mix nil-receiver semantics (silent no-op/zero return in `UpdateBroadcast`/`GetState`) with hard errors in `UpdateScheduledPack`/`PublishSchedulePack`, making precondition contracts non-uniform (`goseg/broadcast/state.go`).

WiFi methods expose a dual-result contract (data + warning error) that is only partially honored by internal callers: `primaryWifiDevice` downgrades partial parse errors, but `listSSIDs` forwards raw warning errors unchanged (`goseg/system/wifi_runtime_methods.go`, `goseg/system/wifi_test.go`).

Backpressure/error propagation is explicit in event publishing (`ErrTransitionBusFull`, `ErrTransitionPublishTimeout`) but absent in system log publishing (`PublishSystemLog` has no error return despite asynchronous bus semantics), creating inconsistent failure visibility for queue-based operations (`goseg/docker/events/events.go`, `goseg/session/logstream_session_store.go`, `goseg/logger/logger_test.go`).

Config patch parsing mixes strict concrete-type assertions (`parseSessionMap`, `parseDiskWarningMap`) with tolerant JSON re-decode parsing (`parseLinuxUpdatesValue`, `parsePenpaiModels`) inside the same `map[string]interface{}` patch pipeline, producing inconsistent parse error behavior for similarly sourced payloads (`goseg/config/config_update_schema.go`).

WiFi parsing uses partial-parse warnings as `error` while still returning data, but higher-level method handling is inconsistent, so equivalent malformed-input cases can either continue or fail depending on call path (`goseg/system/wifi_runtime_methods.go`, `goseg/system/wifi_test.go`).

findings.summary: Orchestration runtime composition mixes unrelated responsibilities into a single mega-runtime pattern that obscures change boundaries.
evidence: runtime_ops.go defines many unrelated ops groups (container, startup, urbit, startram, health, snapshots, files, context, timers) and also constructs defaults for them in the same unit.
runtime_core.go composes these large embedded structs and merges overrides across all groups, creating broad implicit coupling for any runtime change.
startup_orchestrator_test.go uses custom runtime builders/options to manage startup behavior, indicating callers must understand broad runtime wiring rather than focused module contracts.
findings.suggestion: Split orchestration runtime into narrower subsystem runtimes (for example container lifecycle, startup bootstrap, urbit config, startram integration) with explicit constructor dependencies; keep a thin top-level assembler that wires them together once.

findings.summary: WiFi runtime file conflates parsing, process execution, networking operations, and system policy mutation.
evidence: wifi_runtime_methods.go includes nmcli record parsing helpers, wifi.Client interaction, shell command execution, link toggling, and captive sysctl writes in one implementation surface.
wifi_test.go has to override multiple low-level seams to test simple device/SSID behaviors, showing test setup is coupled to mixed concerns.
wifi_c2c_flow_test.go separately re-stubs higher-level connection flow behavior, indicating no clean boundary between command parsing/runtime IO and mode orchestration.
findings.suggestion: Extract dedicated components: nmcli parser, command executor adapter, wifi-device service, and captive-rule applier; keep wifiRuntime as a coordinator with narrow interfaces so tests can target each concern independently.

findings.summary: Broadcast state runtime couples concurrency/event responsibilities with brittle manual deep-copy ownership of a large payload model.
evidence: state.go handles lock-protected state mutation, schedule bus publication, collector dispatch, and a large hand-maintained cloneBroadcastState function.
state_test.go contains broad mutation tests to guard clone behavior across many nested fields, showing the clone contract is spread and fragile.
The payload model surface is large and evolving; manual clone logic in one runtime requires constant synchronized updates across representation and tests.
findings.suggestion: Move state ownership to a focused store and encapsulate payload copy semantics behind typed clone/copy methods closer to model definitions, or switch to immutable update helpers that eliminate manual deep-copy maintenance in runtime logic.

findings.summary: Config patch schema module is overloaded with schema, parsing, application, merge, and boot-time registry concerns.
evidence: config_update_schema.go defines patch shapes, generic field descriptors, parse functions, apply functions, merge helpers, global registry maps, and init-time validation in one file.
config_test.go carries very large end-to-end merge/patch scenarios, suggesting behavior is difficult to reason about in smaller isolated units.
The combination of global init registry state and mixed parse/apply/merge duties increases cognitive load and makes extension of config keys error-prone.
findings.suggestion: Separate the module into distinct layers: field catalog definition, parsing/validation, apply engine, and merge policy; replace init-time global registry with explicit constructor-built registries used by callers/tests.

findings.summary: Runtime dependency methods use conflicting nil/missing-callback contracts (silent success vs explicit error) within the same API families.
evidence: `StartupRuntime.Initialize()` returns `nil` when `InitializeFn` is unset, but sibling methods return `seams.MissingRuntimeDependency(...)` on unset callbacks.
`broadcastStateRuntime` nil receiver returns zero/no-op for `GetState` and `UpdateBroadcast`, but returns `errBroadcastRuntimeRequired` for `UpdateScheduledPack` and `PublishSchedulePack`.
`logstreamSessionStore` methods uniformly no-op on nil receiver, including mutating/publishing paths, with no error path available.
findings.suggestion: Define and enforce one runtime precondition policy per API family (either explicit dependency errors or documented no-op semantics), then normalize all methods to that policy and add contract tests for nil/missing dependency behavior.

findings.summary: Asynchronous publish paths expose backpressure/cancellation errors inconsistently across subsystems.
evidence: `publishDropOnFull` returns `ErrTransitionBusFull` and `ErrTransitionPublishTimeout`, giving callers deterministic queue failure signals.
`LogstreamRuntime.PublishSystemLog` returns no error and `logstreamSessionStore.PublishSystemLog` unconditionally publishes with no propagation path.
Logger sink contract in tests (`testSystemLogSink.PublishSystemLog`) assumes fire-and-forget semantics, reinforcing a non-reporting error strategy unlike event publishing.
findings.suggestion: Standardize async publish interfaces around an explicit result contract (e.g., `error` with shared sentinel errors for full/cancelled/not-initialized), or define a single explicit best-effort policy and remove/encapsulate conflicting strict error semantics behind dedicated adapters.

findings.summary: WiFi API methods expose partial data plus warning errors, but callers and sibling methods do not apply a consistent handling strategy.
evidence: `parseNmcliWifiDeviceRecords` and `parseNmcliWifiScanResults` can return populated results with `wifiNmcliPartialParseError`.
`primaryWifiDevice` explicitly downgrades partial parse errors when at least one device exists, while `listSSIDs` directly returns `(ssids, err)` and leaves policy to callers.
Tests encode this dual contract (`wifiDevices` and `listSSIDs` expect non-empty results with non-nil partial-parse errors), indicating a deliberately mixed but hard-to-predict API shape.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a single typed partial-result abstraction (for example `{Data, Warnings}` or a dedicated warning channel/type) and apply it consistently across `wifiDevices`, `listSSIDs`, and upstream call sites so callers do not need method-specific error interpretation.

findings.summary: Config patch parsing applies inconsistent type-tolerance rules for values from the same `map[string]interface{}` boundary.
evidence: `buildConfigPatch` ingests `map[string]interface{}` values, but `parseSessionMap`/`parseDiskWarningMap` require concrete map types (`map[string]structs.SessionInfo` / `map[string]structs.DiskWarning`).
In the same parser layer, `parseLinuxUpdatesValue` and `parsePenpaiModels` normalize generic inputs by marshal/unmarshal, accepting structurally compatible untyped maps/slices.
Existing tests heavily validate merge behavior but do not provide equivalent boundary tests for generic map-backed session/disk-warning patches, leaving this inconsistency unguarded.
findings.suggestion: Unify parser strategy at this boundary: either require strict typed option builders before `buildConfigPatch`, or make all composite-field parsers normalize generic map input through shared decode helpers; add tests that feed untyped JSON-decoded maps for every composite patch key.
